1. 
From your letter and an October 12th conversation I 
had with Alan Ross, a volunteer who works in your distr ct 
office, I understand that the intern program is to be 
funded from two sources: 
the interns will be paid through 
a grant received from California State University at 
Fullerton, and the administrative costs of the program 
(salaries for an administrator and secretary and overhead 
expenses) will be paid by private grants from corporations. 
The interns will be placed in the offices of any 
elected official or government agency in Orange County 
which requests an intern. 
No more than two interns will be 
placed in anyone 
fice. 
The interns will be paid $3.00 
an hour, must co~~it themselves to two semesters or three 
quarters of work, and may not work on political campa 
during the hours they serve as interns. 
You have provided us with several scenar 
and 
US whether or not these situations would create reporting

Political Reform Act.

Act. 
would be re 


Assemblyman Chet Wray 
Page Two 
The Political Reform Act requires payments received by 
an officeholder to be listed as either gifts or campaign 
contributions. 
Gifts are listed on an officeholder's State-
ment of Economic Interests, and campaign contributions on a 
campaign statement. 
(See Government Code Section 84200, et 
seq.,!! for reporting of campaign contributions, and Section 
87200, et seq., for gifts.) 
The term "contribution" is defined by Section 82015 of 
the Act as: 
... a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a pay-
ment of a loan by a third party, or an 
enforceable promise to make a payment except 
to the extent that full and adequate considera-
tion is received unless it is clear from the 
surrounding circumstances that it is not made 
for political purposes .... 
The term "political purposes" as used in that section re 
to more than the traditional campaign notion of attempting to 
influence the voters for or against a candidate in connection 
with an election. 
It also refers to actions made for the 
purpose of assisting the officeholder to perform his duties 
as a politician. 
This interpretation recognizes that elected 
officeholders seek to communicate with constituents and other 
governmental officials as a regular part of their "political" 
activities. 
The Commission ratified this concept of political purpose 
in opinions issued to Mayor Janet Gray Hayes, 1 FPPC Opinions 
210 (No. 75-145, December 4,1975), and Assemblyman Willie 
Brown, 1 FPPC Opinions 67 (No. 75-055, July 2, 1975), in 
which the Commission stated that payments received by the 
officeholders from their committees in connection with their 
official duties were political in nature 
Thus, such payments 
were not reportable as gifts on the officeholder's Statement 
of Economic Interest but rather were reportable on the officer's 
campaign statements. 
The fact that such payments were not 
l/ All statutory references are to 
Government Code unless 
otherwi e 


Asserr~lyman Chet Wray 
Page Three 
used for the express purpose of influenc 
voters did not 
cause the funds to lose their political character and 
personal in nature. 
Moreover, it would make little sense 
as a matter of policy to construe such donations as gifts 
rather than contributions in light of the long established 
practice of officeholders creating committees to raise 
funds for use in connection with their official duties. 
Since we 
lieve the situation outlined in example one is 
very similar to the facts cited 
the above mentioned 
opinions, we conclude that you must report the grants as a 
contribution. 
Your first question is marked 
different from your 
second and third examples in which payments are made to a 
university or a state agency, both of which place interns 
in many legis 1 
and public agency off 
We think 
these latter cases are distinguishable from the first 
three respects. 
First, the grant is made to a university 
or a state agency. 
5e 
• the university or state agency 
has complete control over the 
rn program. 
Third, the 
i 
or state agency administers the internsh 
program 
connection with a bona fide 
onal 
curriculum. 
Further, these interns would be placed in 
all offices in 
County which request them. 
We 
believe this is analogous to the situation in which 
special districts pay for pUblication of all candidates' 
statements of qualification. 
Although the candidate is 
clearly receiving a benefit, this benefit is extended to 
all candidates equally and thus is not considered report-
able under the Political Reform Act. 
See opinion reques 
by H. L. Masini, 2 FPPC Opinions 38 (No. 75-l7l-B, February 
4, 1976). 
We believe your second and third examples would 
result in no reporting obligation for the office in which 
the interns are placed, the university or state agency 
which administers the program, or the entity which 
finances it. 
Our answer would not be different if an administrator 
were paid by a private company provided that the facts out-
lined in 
second and third examples were still operative. 
If the interns took leaves of absences from their case-
work to do campaign work but continued to be paid by the 
program, 
g obligations would be ere 
since such 
payments would be in-kind contributions to the campaign. 
However, we understand your question to mean that the 
interns will not 
to work on campaigns, and 
even if 
be paid for such work. 
1 
t wh ch 
worki 
on 
their of 


Assemblyman Chet Wray 
Page Four 
Finally, if any part of the intern program is financed 
with campaign funds, the relevant campaign committee would 
be obligated to disclose such expenditures. 
However, a 
partial funding of the program by your campaign would not 
change our answer that if the program were open to all 
officials and agencies and was being run by the university 
or a state agency, there would be no reporting requirement 
on any private firm making contributions to the program, the 
university or state agency receiving the contribution, or the 
officials or agencies using the interns. 
Please calIon me if you have any further questions. 
RMS:nc 
Sincerely, 
Robert M. Stern 
General Counsel

sections.question_synthetic: Assemblyman Wray asked whether an intern program with funding from university grants and private corporate grants would create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding whether payments received would be reportable as gifts or campaign contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that if the intern program is administered by a university or state agency and is open to all elected officials and government agencies in Orange County, there is no reporting requirement on the private firms making contributions, the administering entity, or the officials using the interns. However, if grants are made directly to the officeholder's office (first example), they must be reported as campaign contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an FPPC advice letter responding to Opinion Request No. 77-029. No formal sections marked as QUESTION or CONCLUSION, but the letter clearly addresses the inquiry and provides FPPC's analytical response. Minor OCR artifacts present but did not impede extraction of substantive content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Assemblyman Wray asked whether an intern program with funding from university grants and private corporate grants would create reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding whether payments received would be reportable as gifts or campaign contributions.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC concluded that if the intern program is administered by a university or state agency and is open to all elected officials and government agencies in Orange County, there is no reporting requirement on the private firms making contributions, the administering entity, or the officials using the interns. However, if grants are made directly to the officeholder's office (first example), they must be reported as campaign contributions.

embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel Robert Stern advises Assemblyman Chet Wray that an intern program funded through university grants and private corporate donations would have no reporting obligations if administered by a university or state agency and offered equally to all officials, but would require contribution reporting if funds flow directly to the officeholder's office.
